item.title: Home Sweet Home
item.dc:creator: Kendal Hasemann
item.description:
Is There Truly Such Thing as a Home Court Advantage? Let’s see if PBA actually gave the Huskers a one up against their opponents this past basketball season.

Winning on the road is one of the hardest things to do in college basketball. Players face unfamiliar arenas, hostile crowds, and travel fatigue can quickly affect the way a team performs. For Nebraska during the 2025-2026 season, location mattered more than ever.

The Huskers were a strong team overall, but their best basketball was consistently at home. Whether it was shooting efficiency, defensive pressure, or simply just out scoring their opponent. Nebraska looked sharper and stronger when playing at Pinnacle Bank Arena compared to anywhere else.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)

nebball <- read_csv("data/nebraskabasketball2526.csv") |>
  mutate(DATE = mdy(DATE))

home <- nebball |>
  filter(
    LOCATION == "Home"
  )

away <- nebball |>
  filter(
    LOCATION == "Away"
  )

neutral <- nebball |>
  filter(
    LOCATION == "Neutral"
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=home, 
    aes(x=PCT, y=`3PCT`, size=PTS), 
    color="#e41c38", 
    alpha=.8) + 
  geom_point(
    data=away, 
    aes(x=PCT, y=`3PCT`, size=PTS), 
    color="grey",
    alpha=.8) +
geom_point(
    data=neutral, 
    aes(x=PCT, y=`3PCT`, size=PTS), 
    color="black",
    alpha=.8) +
  scale_size(name = "Points") +
  labs(
    title = "Where the Shots Fall: Nebraska’s Shooting by Venue",
    subtitle = "Comparing field goals and three-pointers made at home, away, and neutral sites during the 2025–2026 season",
    x = "Field Goals Made",
    y = "Three Pointers Made",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Basketball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )
Code 
ggsave("image.png")

The clearest sign of Nebraska’s home court advantage comes from its offense. The shooting graph shows that home games were clustered in strong scoring zones, with better field goal percentages and more three-pointers made than most road or neutral site performances. Several of the Huskers’ biggest offensive performances were in Lincoln, where they were able to combine efficient shooting with high point totals.

On the other hand, road games were a little more scattered. Some performances were strong, but overall Nebraska’s shooting was less consistent away from home. Neutral site games were more in the middle, showing flashes of efficiency without the same reliability as home games. Their strong performance offensively most likely came from familiarity with their environment. Teams often shoot better in their own arena because of routine, crowd energy, and increased confidence. Nebraska’s data strongly suggests that was the case.

But scoring efficiency was only part of the story. The Huskers also separated themselves from opponents more by playing more consistently when at home.

Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=nebball, 
    aes(
      y=reorder(OPPONENT, desc(DATE)), yend=OPPONENT,
      x=PTS, xend=OPPONENTPTS),
    color="grey",
    linewidth = 1.5,
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=nebball,
    aes(
      y=OPPONENT,
      x=PTS,
      color="Nebraska Points"
    ), color="#e41c38",
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=nebball,
    aes(
      y=OPPONENT,
      x=OPPONENTPTS,
    color="Opponent Points"
    ), color="black",
    size=2
  ) +
 labs(
    title = "Score by Score: How Nebraska Matched Up Against Opponents",
    subtitle = "Nebraska’s points (red) vs. opponent points (black) in each game of the 2025–2026 season",
    x = "Nebraska's Points vs Opponents' Points",
    y = "",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Basketball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

The second graph compares Nebraska’s points to opponent points for each game. In many match ups, the Huskers’ scoring mark sits above their opponents’. Those larger spreads often appeared during home games where Nebraska was able to control the tempo and create distance on the scoreboard.

When Nebraska traveled, the margins were noticeably tighter. Several road games were close in score, with the Huskers needing late execution rather than early dominance. Even when they won on the road the scoring gap was smaller than many of their home victories.

This trend reinforces the idea that Nebraska didn’t just win more games at home, they won by bigger margins. And the final graph shows how that was possible.

Code 
bar1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=home, aes(x=OPPONENT, weight=AST), fill="#e41c38") + 
  geom_bar(data=away, aes(x=OPPONENT, weight=AST), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=neutral, aes(x=OPPONENT, weight=AST), fill="black") +
  coord_flip() +
    labs(
    title = "Assists",
    subtitle = "Home (red), away (gray), neutral (black).",
    x = "",
    y = "Assists",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Basketball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

bar2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=home, aes(x=OPPONENT, weight=TREB), fill="#e41c38") + 
  geom_bar(data=away, aes(x=OPPONENT, weight=TREB), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=neutral, aes(x=OPPONENT, weight=TREB), fill="black") +
  coord_flip() +
    labs(
    title = "Rebounds",
    subtitle = "Home (red), away (gray), neutral (black).",
    x = "",
    y = "Rebounds",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Basketball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

bar1 + bar2

Basketball games are often decided by possessions and execution. Nebraska’s home numbers in rebounds and assists reveal a team that played with more control and efficiency in Lincoln.

At home, the Huskers regularly posted stronger rebounding totals, creating second chance opportunities while limiting their opponents’. Assist numbers also reflected a smoother offense, where ball movement led to easier buckets.

Those categories matter because they often lead to wins. More rebounds means more possessions and opportunities. More assists means better shots and a more efficient offense.

Together, they paint the picture of a team that was elevated by its environment. Nebraska was strong almost everywhere during the 2025-2026 season, but they were at their best in Lincoln. The Huskers shot better, had more control, and made more winning plays at home.

In a sport where every edge matters, Pinnacle Bank Arena gave Nebraska one of its biggest advantages throughout the entirety of the season.

item.title: Undefeated… Until It Mattered Most
item.dc:creator: Kendal Hasemann
item.description:
Did Nebraska Volleyball Peak Too Early in the 2025 Season? Let’s see what the data has to say.

Upon first glance, people and fans may think that Nebraska Volleyball peaked early in the 2025 season. The offense shows flashes of dominance right out of the gate, with strong hitting percentages being apparent as early as September. But if you take a step back and look at the full season, the story becomes a little more complex.

The Huskers had quite the year, going undefeated in the regular season during their first year with Coach Dani Busboom Kelly. They opened the season with solid hitting performances, including multiple matches where the Huskers hit above .350 and some even pushing into the .400 range. That kind of success early on can give the impression that the team was already at its best.

However, those strong early games aren’t unique for Nebraska. As the season progressed into October and November, Nebraska continued to have equally strong, if not stronger offensive performances, including some of the highest hitting percentages of the whole season. The early games weren’t a peak, it was the beginning of a pattern of high level play.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)

nevb <- read_csv("data/Book1.csv") |>
  mutate(DATE = mdy(DATE))

loss <- nevb |>
  filter(
    DATE == "2025-12-14"
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=nevb, aes(x=DATE, weight=PCT)) + 
  geom_bar(data=loss, aes(x=DATE, weight=PCT), fill="#e41c38") +
  labs(
    title = "Rising and Falling: Tracking The Huskers Offensive Power",
    subtitle = "Nebraska Volleyball's Hitting Percentage Per Match in the 2025 Season",
    x = "Game Date",
    y = "Hitting Percentage",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Volleyball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

While the early season showed off some strong performances, it also showed variability, with some matches dipping closer to .250 or .300. As the season went on, those dips became less frequent, and high efficiency matches appeared more consistently.

This shows that Nebraska didn’t fade after a hot start to the season. Instead, they became more reliable offensively as the season progressed. If the Huskers had peaked early, we would expect to see defensive performance drop off later in the season.

But the relationship between digs and blocks remained relatively steady throughout. There’s no clear decline, just a consistent mix of performances, which tells us that the defense didn’t regress, it stayed stable, but not dominant all year.

Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=nevb, aes(x=DIG, y=TB)) +
  geom_smooth(data=nevb, aes(x=DIG, y=TB), method="lm", color="#e41c38") +
  labs(
    title = "Cornhuskers on Defense: How Blocks and Digs Shape the Game",
    subtitle = "Nebraska Volleyball Digs and Blocks Per Match in the 2025 Season",
    x = "Digs per Match",
    y = "Blocks per Match",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Volleyball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

The kills, blocks, and errors graph reinforces the following, kills remained high throughout the entire season, even showing an increase later in the season. Errors fluctuated, but didn’t spike in a way that suggests a late season collapse. Blocks stayed relatively consistent throughout the season.

Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=nevb, aes(x=DATE, y=K), color="#e41c38") +
  geom_line(data=nevb, aes(x=DATE, y=E), color="grey") +
  geom_line(data=nevb, aes(x=DATE, y=TB), color="black") +
  labs(
    title = "Battle at the Net: Kills, Blocks, and Errors",
    subtitle = "Nebraska Volleyball's Kills (in red), Blocks (in black), and Errors (in gray) Per Match in the 2025 Season",
    x = "Game Date",
    y = "Kills, Blocks, Errors",
    caption = "Source: Nebraska Volleyball | Graphic by Kendal Hasemann"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

So, did Nebraska Peak Early?

The short answer is no.

The Huskers didn’t peak early. They sustained and even continued to build on their performances over the course of the 2025 season. They had a strong start and continued to play high level volleyball. They ended their season with an impressive 33-1. It was a year of growth and trying new things. It’s a season the girls should be proud of.

Nebraska’s strength and consistency carried them to an undefeated regular season, proving their offense and overall performance stayed steady from beginning to end. But the data also showed a vulnerability of theirs, inconsistency in errors and a defense that was steady, yet wasn’t always dominant.

In the end, that caught up to them. When Nebraska faced off against Texas A&M in the Regional Final of the 2025 NCAA Women’s Volleyball Tournament, they fell short, losing 2 sets to 3 in a heartbreaker that ended their season.

The Huskers weren’t a team that faded out after a strong start to their season. They were a team whose strengths carried them far, but whose small weaknesses ultimately made the difference when it mattered most.
